1: The superscription assigns Psalm 68 to David as both a psalm (mizmôr) and a song (shîr). The opening words are a direct quotation of the ark-setting-out formula from Num 10:35: יָקוּם אֱלֹהִים יָפוּצוּ אוֹיְבָיו (yāqûm ʾĕlōhîm yāpûṣû ʾôyĕbāyw, "Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered"). In Numbers the formula was spoken by Moses each time the ark moved through the wilderness; its citation here signals that Psalm 68 is a processional hymn accompanying the ark into the sanctuary. Qûm (to arise — the standing-up from rest of a king about to act): God roused to march. וְיָנוּסוּ מְשַׂנְאָיו מִפָּנָיו (wĕyānûsû mĕśānʾāyw mippānāyw, "let those who hate him flee before him") — the enemies in full flight before the advancing divine presence. The ark was the visible throne of the invisible God; when it moved, the enemies' flight was anticipated as already accomplished.
2: Two similes for the dissolution of the wicked: כְּהִנְדֹּף עָשָׁן תִּנְדֹּף (kĕhindōp ʿāshān tindōp, "as smoke is dispersed by the wind, disperse them") — nādap: to blow away, to scatter (the insubstantial enemy driven off by a gust). כְּהִמֵּס דּוֹנַג מִפְּנֵי-אֵשׁ (kĕhimmês dônag mippĕnê-ʾêsh, "as wax melts before fire") — māsas (niphal: to melt, to dissolve): wax before fire is the image of swift, irreversible dissolution. Both similes are chosen for their speed: smoke is gone in a gust; wax melts at the fire's approach. The enemies are already as insubstantial as smoke, already as fugitive as wax. יֹאבְדוּ רְשָׁעִים מִפְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים (yōʾbĕdû rĕshāʿîm mippĕnê ʾĕlōhîm, "so let the wicked perish before God") — ʾābad: to be destroyed (as in Ps 1:6 — the way of the wicked perishes).
3: וְצַדִּיקִים יִשְׂמְחוּ יַעַלְצוּ לִפְנֵי אֱלֹהִים (wĕṣaddîqîm yiśmĕḥû yaʿalṣû lipnê ʾĕlōhîm, "But let the righteous be glad and rejoice before God") — two joy verbs: śāmaḥ (to be glad — the general term) and ʿālaṣ (to exult, to leap with joy — the more physical, dancing delight). וְיָשִׂישׂוּ בְשִׂמְחָה (wĕyāśîśû bĕśimḥāh, "let them celebrate with great joy") — śîś (to exult greatly — Isa 61:10: "I will greatly rejoice in the LORD"). Three joy terms accumulate: the righteous response to God's arising is an escalating, irrepressible delight. Contrast with the enemies' three-stage dissolution (smoke, wax, perishing).
4: שִׁירוּ לֵאלֹהִים זַמְּרוּ שְׁמוֹ (shîrû lêʾlōhîm zammĕrû shĕmô, "Sing to God, sing praises to his name") — the double call to song: shîr (vocal song) and zāmar (instrumental praise). סֹלּוּ לָרֹכֵב בָּעֲרָבוֹת (sōllû lārōkêb bāʿărābôt, "exalt him who rides upon the ʿărābôt") — sālal: to heap up, to lift up, to make a highway for. Rōkêb bāʿărābôt: "rider on the ʿărābôt" — "the heavens" (cf. Deut 33:26). This epithet directly parallels the Ugaritic divine title rkb ʿrpt ("rider on the clouds") applied to Baal in Canaanite literature — the Psalmist claims that title exclusively for YHWH, performing a polemical displacement of the storm-god. בְּיָהּ שְׁמוֹ (bĕYāh shĕmô, "the LORD [JAH] is his name") — Yāh: the contracted form of YHWH (יָהּ), appearing independently only here and in the hallĕlû-Yāh formula. The short divine name asserts the identity of the heavens-rider with Israel's covenant God.
5: אֲבִי יְתוֹמִים וְדַיַּן אַלְמָנוֹת (ʾăbî yĕtômîm wĕdayyān ʾalmānôt, "a father of orphans and a defender of widows") — two divine titles for those without natural protectors: ʾāb (father) to the yātôm (orphan — the fatherless); dayyān (judge/defender/advocate — from dîn: to govern, to plead a cause, to defend in court) for the ʾalmānāh (widow — the one without a husband-protector). Together they define the divine character as the advocate for the legally and socially voiceless — the theme of Exod 22:22-24; Deut 10:18; Isa 1:17. אֱלֹהִים בִּמְעוֹן קָדְשׁוֹ (ʾĕlōhîm bimʿôn qodshô, "God in his holy dwelling") — from the holy dwelling above, God attends to those below who have no earthly advocate.
6: אֱלֹהִים מוֹשִׁיב יְחִידִים בַּיְתָה (ʾĕlōhîm môshîb yĕḥîdîm baytāh, "God gives the isolated a home to belong to") — yāḥîd: solitary, isolated, alone (the single one without family network). Môshîb (hiphil participle: the one who settles, who causes to dwell): God as the divine settlor who plants the isolated into a household. מוֹצִיא אֲסִירִים בַּכּוֹשָׁרוֹת (môṣîʾ ʾăsîrîm bakkôshārôt, "he brings prisoners out into freedom") — bakkôshārôt: possibly "with singing/with prosperity" (from kāshar: to prosper) — the liberation accompanied by flourishing. אַךְ סוֹרְרִים שָׁכְנוּ צְחִיחָה (ʾak sôrĕrîm shākĕnû ṣĕḥîḥāh, "but the rebellious dwell in a parched land") — sôrēr: the stubborn rebel (Deut 21:18); ṣĕḥîḥāh: a scorched, sun-baked waste — the habitat that mirrors the spiritual condition of those who resist the settling God.
7: אֱלֹהִים בְּצֵאתְךָ לִפְנֵי עַמֶּךָ (ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣêtĕkā lipnê ʿammekā, "O God, when you went out before your people") — the theophany-march begins: God going before Israel (Exod 13:21-22; Num 10:33). בְּצַעְדְּךָ בִישִׁימוֹן סֶלָה (bĕṣaʿdĕkā bîshîmôn selāh, "when you marched through the wilderness") — ṣāʿad: to march, to stride (the measured military step of a warrior); yĕshîmôn: the desolate wilderness (Num 21:20). The phrase closely parallels the Song of Deborah (Judg 5:4-5): "LORD, when you went out from Seir... the earth trembled." Both texts preserve memory of the same wilderness march. Selāh marks the pause before the theophany description.
8: אֶרֶץ רָעָשָׁה אַף-שָׁמַיִם נָטְפוּ (ʾereṣ rāʿāshāh ʾap-shāmayim nāṭĕpû, "the earth shook, and the heavens poured down rain") — rāʿash: to quake, to tremble (the seismic response to divine approach — Ps 77:18; Judg 5:4); nāṭap: to drip, to pour down. מִפְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים זֶה סִינַי (mippĕnê ʾĕlōhîm zeh sînay, "at the presence of God — even Sinai itself") — the parenthetical identification: Sinai is the paradigm-theophany, and all else trembles by reference to it. מִפְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (mippĕnê ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾêl, "at the presence of God, the God of Israel") — the full divine title anchors the cosmic trembling in covenant history: this is not generic deity but Israel's covenant God.
9: גֶּשֶׁם נְדָבוֹת תָּנִיף אֱלֹהִים (geshem nĕdābôt tānîp ʾĕlōhîm, "You sent an abundant rain, O God") — nĕdābôt: freewill-offerings, voluntary gifts (from nādab: to give freely without compulsion — as in Exod 25:2: the freewill offering for the tabernacle). The rain described as a nĕdābāh is a divine gift given out of liberality, not obligation. Nûp (hiphil: to wave, to shake loose): God scattering the rain from the clouds. נַחֲלָתְךָ וְנִלְאָה אַתָּה כוֹנַנְתָּהּ (naḥălātĕkā wĕnilʾāh ʾattāh kônantāh, "you restored your weary inheritance") — nālaʾ: to be weary, exhausted; kûn (polel: to establish firmly, to restore): God re-establishing the exhausted inheritance. The "inheritance" (naḥălāh) is the covenant people — weary from the wilderness and needing divine restoration.
10: חַיָּתְךָ יָשְׁבוּ-בָהּ (ḥayyātĕkā yāshĕbû-bāh, "Your congregation settled in the land") — ḥayyāh: a community, a living group (the living community of the covenant — from ḥāyāh: to live). תָּכִין בְּטוֹבָתְךָ לֶעָנִי אֱלֹהִים (tākîn bĕṭôbātĕkā leʿānî ʾĕlōhîm, "in your goodness, O God, you provided for the needy") — kûn (hiphil: to establish, to prepare, to provide — as a host sets a table for a guest): God's goodness (ṭôbāh) as the instrument of provision for the ʿānî (the needy, the afflicted — the one who is bent down by want). The land settlement is not merely a political event but an act of divine provision for those who depended entirely on God's goodness.
11: אֲדֹנָי יִתֶּן-אֹמֶר (ʾădōnāy yittên-ʾōmer, "The Lord gave the word") — ʾōmer: a word, a saying (the divine proclamation that sets the victory in motion). הַמְבַשְּׂרוֹת צָבָא רָב (hamĕbaśśĕrôt ṣābāʾ rāb, "great was the army of women who announced the victory") — bāśar (piel feminine plural participle: women announcing/heralding good news): the mĕbaśśĕrôt are women heralds of victory (as in Exod 15:20-21 where Miriam leads the women in the victory song; 1 Sam 18:6-7 where women with timbrels celebrate David's victories). Ṣābāʾ rāb: a great army of female heralds — the victory announcement is carried by an army of women. The divine word given; the women's proclamation as the immediate response.
12: מַלְכֵי צְבָאוֹת יִדֹּדוּן יִדֹּדוּן (malkê ṣĕbāʾôt yiddōdûn yiddōdûn, "The kings of armies fled in haste") — the doubled yiddōdûn marks the frantic speed of the kings' flight. וּנְוַת-בַּיִת תְּחַלֵּק שָׁלָל (ûnĕwat-bayit tĕḥallêq shālāl, "she who stayed at home shared in the plunder") — nĕwat-bayit: "the one who stays at the house" — the woman at home, not on the battlefield, yet given a share in the plunder. This is the custom of Num 31:27: the spoil divided between fighters and the rest of the community. Even those who did not fight receive God's victory-gift.
13: The verse is notoriously difficult. אִם-תִּשְׁכְּבוּן בֵּין שְׁפַתָּיִם (ʾim-tishkĕbûn bên shĕpattayim, "even if you once lay among the sheepfolds") — shĕpattayim: possibly sheepfolds, saddlebags, or the two sides of something — the word's meaning is uncertain. The imagery suggests a former low or resting state. כַּנְפֵי יוֹנָה נֶחְפָּה בַכֶּסֶף (kanpê yônāh neḥpāh bakkesep, "the wings of a dove covered with silver") — the dove with silver-covered wings: possibly Israel emerging from disgrace into the glory of deliverance (Ps 74:19 where the dove represents the community). The contrast is between resting in lowly sheepfolds and the subsequent shining of silver-and-gold wings. וְאֶבְרוֹתֶיהָ בִּירַקְרַק חָרוּץ (wĕʾebrôtêhā bîraqraq ḥārûṣ, "her feathers with yellow gold") — yrq-yrq: reduplication for gleaming yellowish-green; ḥārûṣ: refined gold. The image of a dove whose plain feathers have been transformed by divine victory into gleaming gold — glory following humiliation.
14: בְּפָרֵשׂ שַׁדַּי מְלָכִים בָּהּ (bĕpārêś shadday mĕlākîm bāh, "When the Almighty scattered kings throughout the land") — Shadday: the Almighty (the patriarchal divine name — Gen 17:1; 28:3); pāraś: to spread out, to scatter. תַּשְׁלֵג בְּצַלְמוֹן (tashleg bĕṣalmôn, "it snowed on Zalmon") — shālag (hiphil: to snow — the only occurrence of the hiphil of shālag); Ṣalmôn: a dark or shaded mountain (near Shechem — Judg 9:48). The sudden whitening of the dark mountain may image the transformation of the enemy-landscape by divine power — the darkness of enemy territory blanched white by the Almighty's intervention.
15: הַר-אֱלֹהִים הַר-בָּשָׁן (har-ʾĕlōhîm har-bāshān, "The mountain of God is like the mountain of Bashan") — an exclamation addressed to the Bashan range (including Hermon — 9,232 feet), the most impressive mountain system in the region. הַר-גַּבְנֻנִּים הַר-בָּשָׁן (har-gabnunnîm har-bāshān, "a high, many-peaked mountain, like the mountain of Bashan") — gabnunnîm: many-ridged, jagged-peaked (a word for the serrated ridge-line of a great mountain range). The verse sets up the contrast to follow: Bashan is magnificent, many-peaked, and seemingly the natural choice for a divine dwelling — yet God chose differently.
16: לָמָּה תְּרַצְּדוּן הָרִים גַּבְנֻנִּים (lāmmāh tĕraṣṣĕdûn hārîm gabnunnîm, "Why do you look with envy, O many-peaked mountains?") — rāṣad: to look askance, to eye with jealousy (a rare verb — the mountains personified as looking with competitive envy at Zion). הָהָר חָמַד אֱלֹהִים לְשִׁבְתּוֹ (hāhār ḥāmad ʾĕlōhîm lĕshibtô, "This is the mountain that God desired for his dwelling") — ḥāmad: to desire, to covet, to delight in (the same root as the Tenth Commandment's lōʾ taḥmōd). God's sovereign desire chose Zion — the smaller, less dramatic hill — over the imposing Bashan range. אַף-יְהוָה יִשְׁכֹּן לָנֶצַח (ʾap-yĕhwāh yishkōn lāneṣaḥ, "the LORD will dwell there forever") — the permanence of the divine choice: Zion as the eternal dwelling, not by geological majesty but by divine election (Deut 7:7-8: God chose Israel not because of its greatness but because of love).
17: רֶכֶב אֱלֹהִים רִבֹּתַיִם אַלְפֵי שִׁנְאָן (rekeb ʾĕlōhîm ribbōtayim ʾalpê shinʾān, "The chariots of God are twenty thousands, thousands upon thousands") — ribbōtayim: the dual of ten-thousand = twenty thousand; ʾalpê shinʾān: "thousands upon thousands" (the exact meaning of shinʾān is debated: possibly "repeated," "of angels," or "of the terrifying"). The massive enumeration of the divine army dwarfs any human force. אֲדֹנָי בָם סִינַי בַּקֹּדֶשׁ (ʾădōnāy bām sînay baqqōdesh, "the Lord is among them — as in Sinai, in the holy place") — the key theological claim: the same divine presence that appeared at Sinai in the wilderness is now present in Zion's sanctuary. Sinai-holiness and Zion-holiness are continuous; the God of the wilderness theophany has brought his throne permanently to Jerusalem.
18: The most theologically freighted verse in the psalm and the one cited in the NT. עָלִיתָ לַמָּרוֹם שָׁבִיתָ שֶּׁבִי (ʿālîtā lammārôm shābîtā shebî, "You have ascended to the heights, leading a train of captives") — ʿālāh: to ascend; shābāh shebî: the cognate-accusative construction "you led captivity captive" (the absolute internal object — emphasizing the completeness and finality of the victory). The image is of the victorious king ascending to his throne after battle, bringing the conquered enemies as the trophy of his triumph. לָקַחְתָּ מַתָּנוֹת בָּאָדָם (lāqaḥtā mattānôt bāʾādām, "you have received tribute from men") — the MT reads "received/taken" (lāqaḥtā): the victorious king receives tribute. Eph 4:8 quotes this with "he gave gifts to men" (ἔδωκεν δόματα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις) — reversing the direction. Paul's reading either follows a targum tradition or applies a midrashic technique: the ascended Christ distributes to the church what he received; the "giving" is the Spirit's gifts given through the ascended Lord. The reversal is deliberate: Christ did not ascend to hoard the tribute but to distribute the Spirit as gifts. אַף סוֹרְרִים לִשְׁכֹּן יָהּ אֱלֹהִים (ʾap sôrĕrîm lishkōn Yāh ʾĕlōhîm, "yes, even the rebellious — that the LORD God might dwell there") — the extraordinary extension: the victory benefits even the formerly rebellious.
19: בָּרוּךְ אֲדֹנָי יוֹם יוֹם (bārûk ʾădōnāy yôm yôm, "Blessed be the Lord, who day after day carries our burdens for us") — the distributive repetition yôm yôm (day day = every single day): the divine carrying is not a one-time rescue but a continuous, daily burden-bearing. יַעֲמָס-לָנוּ הָאֵל יְשׁוּעָתֵנוּ סֶלָה (yaʿămās-lānû hāʾêl yĕshûʿātênû selāh, "the God of our salvation carries our loads") — ʿāmas: to load, to carry a burden as a beast of burden carries a pack (Isa 46:1-4: "I have carried you from before your birth... I will carry and will save"). God compared to a beast of burden lovingly carrying what his people cannot carry themselves. The warrior-God of the processional is also the daily burden-bearer. Selāh.
20: הָאֵל לָנוּ אֵל לְמוֹשָׁעוֹת (hāʾêl lānû ʾêl lĕmôshāʿôt, "Our God is the God of deliverances") — môshāʿôt: plural of salvation/deliverance: multiple deliverances, the accumulated repertoire of divine rescues. The God of one salvation would be remarkable; the God of deliverances (plural) is the God of a track record. וְלֵיהוִה אֲדֹנָי לַמָּוֶת תּוֹצָאוֹת (wĕlayĕhwāh ʾădōnāy lammāwet tôṣāʾôt, "and to the LORD, the Lord, belong the ways of escape from death") — tôṣāʾôt: exits, escape routes (from yāṣāʾ: to go out). Even from death — the ultimate closure — God has exits. The plural tôṣāʾôt suggests multiple escape routes that God holds: he who controls even death's exits controls everything.
21: אַךְ-אֱלֹהִים יִמְחַץ רֹאשׁ אֹיְבָיו (ʾak-ʾĕlōhîm yimḥaṣ rōʾsh ʾôyĕbāyw, "But God will crush the head of his enemies") — māḥaṣ: to shatter, to smash (as in Num 24:17: the star from Jacob "shall crush the foreheads of Moab"; Ps 110:6: "he will shatter heads over the wide earth"). קָדְקֹד שֵׂעָר מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּאֲשָׁמָיו (qodqōd śēʿār mithallêk bāʾăshāmāyw, "the proud scalp of those who persist in their guilt") — qodqōd: the crown/top of the head; śēʿār: hairy (the flowing hair of the unashamed proud — possibly the warrior's long hair). Mithallêk bāʾăshāmāyw: "walking about in their guilt" — those who continue in their offenses without turning. The crushing is directed specifically at the proud who persist in guilt rather than repenting.
22: אָמַר אֲדֹנָי מִבָּשָׁן אָשִׁיב (ʾāmar ʾădōnāy mibbāshān ʾāshîb, "The Lord declared, 'I will bring them back from Bashan'") — the divine speech: bringing back the enemy from Bashan (the far territory east of the Jordan — a general term for the remote places of enemy retreat). אָשִׁיב מִמְּצֻלוֹת יָם (ʾāshîb mimmĕṣûlôt yām, "I will bring my people back from the depths of the sea") — the two extreme geographies: the high territory of Bashan and the depths of the sea. No enemy refuge is beyond God's reach; no oppressed people too deeply submerged to be retrieved. The motif echoes Amos 9:2-3: no depth or height places anyone beyond God's hand.
23: לְמַעַן תִּמְחַץ רַגְלְךָ בְּדָם (lĕmaʿan timḥaṣ raglĕkā bĕdām, "so that your foot will be stained in the blood of your enemies") — a graphic battle image: the victory so complete that the returning warrior's foot is bloodied by the field of the slain. לְשׁוֹן כְּלָבֶיךָ מֵאֹיְבִים מִנֵּהוּ (lĕshôn kĕlābêkā mêʾōyĕbîm minnehû, "and the tongues of your dogs will have their share") — the dogs licking the blood of the battlefield slain (as in 1 Kgs 22:38; 21:19). The image does not celebrate cruelty but the completeness of divine vindication over the enemy: even the scavenging dogs are fed by the thoroughness of the victory.
24: רָאוּ הֲלִיכוֹתֶיךָ אֱלֹהִים (rāʾû hălîkôtêkā ʾĕlōhîm, "People have witnessed your processions, O God") — hălîkāh: procession, march (from hālak: to walk/go). The psalm shifts from narrative of past victories to the present liturgical procession. הֲלִיכוֹת אֵלִי מַלְכִּי בַקֹּדֶשׁ (hălîkôt ʾêlî malkî baqqōdesh, "the processions of my God, my King, into the holy place") — the processional into the sanctuary: the ark brought into the Jerusalem temple, carrying with it the memory of all the Sinai and wilderness marches now culminating in permanent holy dwelling. The worshiper's personal claim: "my God, my King."
25: קִדְּמוּ שָׁרִים אַחַר נֹגְנִים (qiddĕmû shārîm ʾaḥar nōgĕnîm, "The singers lead the procession; behind them come the musicians") — qādam (piel: to go before, to lead): the singers at the head; nōgĕnîm: players of stringed instruments (from nāgan: to play — 1 Sam 16:16-18 for David playing for Saul). בְּתוֹךְ עֲלָמוֹת תּוֹפֵפוֹת (bĕtôk ʿălāmôt tôpĕpôt, "among them, young women playing tambourines") — ʿălāmôt: young women (as in 1 Sam 18:6-7; Exod 15:20: Miriam and the women with timbrels); tôpēp: to beat the tambourine. The procession composition: male singers leading, then string players, with young women with timbrels among them — the full worship orchestra in procession.
26: בְּמַקְהֵלוֹת בָּרְכוּ אֱלֹהִים (bĕmaqhêlôt bārĕkû ʾĕlōhîm, "Bless God in the great assembly") — maqhêlôt: congregations, choral assemblies (from qāhal: the assembled congregation). The call to corporate blessing (bārak): the assembled multitude joining in the doxology. אֲדֹנָי מִמְּקוֹר יִשְׂרָאֵל (ʾădōnāy mimmĕqôr yiśrāʾêl, "praise the LORD, all you who come from the fountain of Israel") — māqôr: spring, fountain, source (Israel's patriarchal origin — the fountain from which all Israel flows). The comprehensive summons: all who trace their origin to the source of Israel are called to join the assembly-blessing.
27: שָׁם בִּנְיָמִן צָעִיר רֹדֵם (shām binyāmîn ṣāʿîr rōdêm, "There is Benjamin, the youngest, leading the way") — ṣāʿîr: the youngest, the smallest (the tribe of Benjamin, the last-born son of Jacob). Benjamin paradoxically leads despite being the smallest. The tribes named in the processional: שָׂרֵי יְהוּדָה רִגְמָתָם (śārê yĕhûdāh rigmātām, "the princes of Judah in their bright company") — rigmāh: a heap, a crowd (a gathered throng). Judah, Zebulun, Naphtali — a representative sample of the northern and southern tribes together, united in the processional worship. The tribal gathering in Jerusalem is the enacted unity of what will be divided under Solomon's successors.
28: צִוָּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ עֻזֶּךָ (ṣiwwāh ʾĕlōhêkā ʿuzzekā, "Your God has commanded your strength") — ṣāwāh: to command, to ordain (God having already ordained and established the people's strength — the strength is not self-generated but divinely decreed). עוּזָּה אֱלֹהִים זוּ פָּעַלְתָּ לָּנוּ (ʿûzzāh ʾĕlōhîm zû pāʿaltā llānû, "show your strength, O God, in what you have already begun for us") — the petition: not for new power but for the continuation and display of what God has already done. The perfect pāʿaltā ("what you have done") grounds the imperative ʿûzzāh ("show strength") — past acts are the basis for present appeal.
29: מֵהֵיכָלֶךָ עַל-יְרוּשָׁלִָם (mêhêkālekā ʿal-yĕrûshālāim, "Because of your temple in Jerusalem") — the temple as the address from which tribute is commanded: the temple's presence in Jerusalem is the magnet drawing the kings. לְךָ יוֹבִילוּ מְלָכִים שָׁי (lĕkā yôbîlû mĕlākîm shāy, "kings will bring their gifts to you") — shāy: a gift, tribute (a rare word — Isa 18:7: "At that time tribute will be brought to the LORD of hosts"). The eschatological vision: the nations' kings bringing their tribute to Jerusalem's temple — developed in Isa 60:3 and Rev 21:24-26.
30: גְּעַר חַיַּת קָנֶה (gĕʿar ḥayyat qāneh, "Rebuke the beast that lurks in the reeds") — gāʿar: to rebuke (the authoritative command of the sovereign); ḥayyat qāneh: "the beast of the reeds" — likely the hippopotamus or crocodile (symbols of Egypt — cf. Ps 74:14: the Leviathan of Egypt; Ezek 29:3: the great dragon lying in the Nile). עֲדַת אַבִּירִים בְּעֶגְלֵי עַמִּים (ʿădat ʾabbîrîm bĕʿeglê ʿammîm, "the herd of bulls among the peoples' young") — the foreign powers as bulls and calves: the strong nations surrounding weak ones. מִתְרַפֵּס בְּרַצֵּי-כָסֶף (mitrappês bĕraṣṣê-khāsep, "until they submit with pieces of silver") — rāpas (hithpael: to trample oneself down, to grovel, to prostrate): forced submission with tribute. בִּזַּר עַמִּים קְרָבוֹת יֶחְפָּצוּ (bizzar ʿammîm qĕrābôt yeḥpāṣû, "scatter the peoples who delight in war") — the divine rebuke scattering the war-delighting nations.
31: יֶאֱתָיוּ חַשְׁמַנִּים מִנִּי מִצְרָיִם (yeʾĕtāyû ḥashmannîm minnî miṣrāyim, "Envoys will come from Egypt") — ḥashmannîm: dignitaries, princes, envoys (a rare word — possibly from an Egyptian loan-word; the nobles who make diplomatic submission). Egypt as the paradigm-oppressor making voluntary submission to God. כּוּשׁ תָּרִיץ יָדָיו לֵאלֹהִים (kûsh tārîṣ yādāyw lêʾlōhîm, "Ethiopia will quickly extend her hands to God") — rûṣ (hiphil: to cause to run, to stretch out quickly): Cush/Ethiopia extending hands in submission and worship. Isaiah 18:7 and 45:14 develop the same vision. The two great African powers of the ancient world submitting to Israel's God: the eschatological horizon of the psalm's processional.
32: מַמְלְכוֹת הָאָרֶץ שִׁירוּ לֵאלֹהִים (mamlaḵôt hāʾāreṣ shîrû lêʾlōhîm, "Sing to God, O kingdoms of the earth") — the summons broadens to all kingdoms: not Israel alone but the mamlaḵôt hāʾāreṣ. The processional that began with the ark on Zion now encompasses the whole earth. זַמְּרוּ אֲדֹנָי סֶלָה (zammĕrû ʾădōnāy selāh, "sing praises to the Lord, Selah") — zāmar: to praise with instruments; Selāh marks the pause before the final doxological section.
33: לָרֹכֵב בִּשְׁמֵי שְׁמֵי-קֶדֶם (lārōkêb bishĕmê shĕmê-qedem, "to him who rides upon the ancient heavens") — shĕmê qedem: "the heavens of old/antiquity" — the primordial heavens (Deut 33:27: "The eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms"). The rider on the ʿărābôt (v4) is now the rider on the ancient heavens — both eternity (time) and the highest heavens (space) are under his sovereignty. הֵן יִתֵּן בְּקוֹלוֹ קוֹל עֹז (hên yittên bĕqôlô qôl ʿōz, "listen, he thunders with his mighty voice") — qôl ʿōz: the powerful voice (Ps 29:3-9 — the voice of the LORD over the waters, shattering cedars). The thunder as the divine speech addressed to all the earth.
34: תְּנוּ עֹז לֵאלֹהִים (tĕnû ʿōz lêʾlōhîm, "Ascribe power to God") — nātan ʿōz: to "give" power — not that God receives power from human declaration, but that the assembly acknowledges and ascribes to God the power that is already his (Ps 29:1-2: "Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength"). עַל-יִשְׂרָאֵל גַּאֲוָתוֹ (ʿal-yiśrāʾêl gaʾăwātô, "his splendor is over Israel") — gaʾăwāh: majesty, splendor (the divine majesty — Exod 15:7: "In the greatness of your majesty you overthrow your adversaries"). God's majesty displayed specifically over Israel — the chosen people as the theater of divine splendor. וְעֻזּוֹ בַּשְּׁחָקִים (wĕʿuzzô bashĕḥāqîm, "and his strength is in the heavens") — shĕḥāqîm: the sky-dust, the clouds/heavens (from shāḥaq: to rub fine).
35: נוֹרָא אֱלֹהִים מִמִּקְדָּשֶׁיךָ (nôrāʾ ʾĕlōhîm mimmiqddāshêkā, "God is awe-inspiring from his sanctuary") — nôrāʾ: fearful, awesome (as in Ps 65:5 — the God of awesome deeds). The processional psalm ends where it began — at the sanctuary, the place of God's dwelling. אֵל יִשְׂרָאֵל הוּא נֹתֵן עֹז וְתַעֲצֻמוֹת לָעָם (ʾêl yiśrāʾêl hûʾ nōtên ʿōz wĕtaʿăṣumôt lāʿām, "the God of Israel is the one who gives strength and power to the people") — taʿăṣumôt: mighty forces, powers (from ʿāṣam: to be strong — the abstract noun for concentrated power). The God asked to "show your strength" (v28) is revealed as the one who gives strength and power to those who worship. בָּרוּךְ אֱלֹהִים (bārûk ʾĕlōhîm, "Blessed be God") — the closing doxology bookending v19's "Blessed be the Lord."
